function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Controlled-company status affects governance requirements but not necessarily economic rights. Distinguish between governance control and economic ownership using the filing’s dual-class structure and board-election mechanics.

steps.reasoning_content: The user is asking about the distinction between governance control and economic ownership in SpaceX's S-1 filing, specifically regarding the dual-class structure and board-election mechanics. Let me first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieve relevant information about the controlled-company status, dual-class share structure, and board election mechanics.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about the dual-class structure, controlled-company status, and board election mechanics from the filing.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the dual-class share structure details including the voting rights per share for each class of stock (Class A, Class B, etc.), the number of shares outstanding for each class, and any supervoting or special voting rights provisions. Also retrieve information about who controls the voting power and the percentage of voting power held by insiders or founders.

In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the controlled-company disclosure including the definition of controlled company, who qualifies as the controlling shareholder, the board composition and election mechanics, and any exemptions from corporate governance requirements claimed under controlled-company status (such as exemptions from independent board, independent committee, or independent director nomination requirements).

In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the board of directors election mechanics including whether directors are elected by plurality vote, majority vote, staggered board or classified board provisions, cumulative voting rights, and how the controlling shareholder's voting power affects board composition. Also retrieve any information about director nomination rights of significant shareholders.

results.content:
# SpaceX Dual-Class Share Structure: Complete Summary

## Classes of Stock

SpaceX has established a **multi-class** common stock structure (plus preferred stock):

| Class | Par Value | Voting Rights |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| **Class A** | $0.001/share | **1 vote per share** |
| **Class B** | $0.001/share | **10 votes per share** (supervoting) |
| **Class C** | $0.001/share | **No voting rights** (except as required by law) |
| **Class D** | To be established | Voting rights to be determined when/if issued by the Board |
| **Preferred Stock** | $0.001/share | Variable; includes Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock |

---

## Authorized Share Counts (Post-IPO)

| Class | Authorized Shares |
|-------|-------------------|
| Class A | **36,132,150,000** |
| Class B | **6,125,000,000** |
| Class C | **10,000,000,000** |
| Preferred Stock | **2,400,000,000** |
| Class D | Not specified in the provided excerpts |

*(Specific issued-and-outstanding figures are left blank in the prospectus, indicating they were to be filled in at final pricing.)*

---

## Voting Structure and Board Control

### Board Election
- **Class B Directors (51%):** Holders of Class B common stock, **voting separately as a class**, elect **51% of the total authorized directors** (rounded up to the nearest whole number). For an 8-person board, this means **5 Class B Directors**.
- **Common Stock Directors (49%):** Holders of **all classes** of voting common stock (A and B), voting together as a single class, elect the remaining directors.
- **No cumulative voting** is permitted.

### Expected Initial Board Composition (8 directors)
- **Class B Directors (5):** Elon Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, Antonio J. Gracias, Donald Harrison, Luke Nosek
- **Common Stock Directors (3):** Ira Ehrenpreis, Randy Glein, Steve Jurvetson

### Special Class B Voting Rights
Class B shareholders must **vote separately as a class** to approve:
1. **Removal of Elon Musk** from his board and leadership roles (CEO and Chairman)
2. Any charter amendment changing the **rights, powers, preferences, or privileges** of Class B common stock (including Class B Director provisions)
3. Certain **combinations, mergers, or sales** as described in the charter

---

## Insider/Founder Voting Power

### Elon Musk
| Metric | Pre-Offering | Post-Offering |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Class A shares | 849,494,440 (12.3%) | Data redacted (listed as blanks) |
| Class B shares | 5,569,053,075 (93.6%) | Data redacted (listed as blanks) |
| **Combined voting power** | **85.1%** | **~XX%** (to be filled at pricing) |

- Musk controls approximately **85.1% of combined voting power** pre-offering.
- Post-IPO, he is expected to hold approximately **[XX]%** of total voting power (percentage redacted in the prospectus pending final pricing).
- He **controls the election of the entire board** — both Class B Directors (directly through Class B ownership) and Common Stock Directors (through majority combined voting power).

### Other Insiders

| Name | Class A Shares | Class B Shares |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Gwynne Shotwell | 5,460,400 | 7,113,550 |
| Bret Johnsen | 9,583,690 | — |
| Ira Ehrenpreis | 809,050 | 564,650 |
| Randy Glein | 277,800 | — |
| Antonio J. Gracias | 503,414,530 | — |
| Donald Harrison | — | — |
| Steve Jurvetson | — | — |
| Luke Nosek | 32,987,360 | — |

---

## Class B Share Conversion Mechanics

- **Voluntary conversion:** Each Class B share is **convertible at any time** at the holder's option into one share of Class A.
- **Automatic conversion:** Each Class B share **automatically converts** into one Class A share upon any **Transfer** of that Class B share (whether or not for value).
- **Exceptions ("Permitted Transfers"):** Certain inter-family transfers and transfers to qualifying entities do **not** trigger automatic conversion.
- **Over time effect:** The conversion mechanism means that as Class B holders sell or transfer shares, their voting power erodes — but those who **retain** their Class B shares see their **relative voting power increase**.

---

## Controlled Company Status

- SpaceX qualifies as a **"controlled company"** under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules following the IPO.
- As a controlled company, SpaceX is **exempt** from requirements to have:
  - A majority-independent board
  - Independent compensation and nominating committees
- SpaceX **remains subject** to the requirement that its **audit committee** be composed entirely of independent directors.

---

## Anti-Takeover Provisions

1. **No cumulative voting** in director elections
2. **Special meetings** can only be called by: the chairman, CEO, president, the board, the founder, or shareholders holding ≥50% of outstanding voting shares
3. **Written consent actions** require at least the minimum vote threshold that would be needed at a meeting
4. **Texas Business Organizations Code §21.606** applies: prohibits 3-year business combinations with "affiliated shareholders" absent board pre-approval or a supermajority shareholder vote
5. **Separate class votes waived** under TBOC for most matters (except the specific Class B protections listed above)

---

## Reserved Shares for Conversions and Equity Compensation (as of March 31, 2026)

| Purpose | Class A | Class B | Class C | Class D |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Redeemable Convertible PS (low-vote) | 3,448 | — | 3,448 | — |
| Redeemable Convertible PS (high-vote) | 3,274 | 3,274 | 3,274 | — |
| Outstanding Class B | 2,421 | — | — | — |
| Outstanding stock options | 8 | 450 | 476 | — |
| Outstanding RSUs | 49 | 1 | 79 | — |
| Future equity grants | 150 | — | 350 | — |
| **Total** | **9,350** | **3,725** | **7,627** | **—** |

*(Figures in thousands or the unit specified in the original filing.)*

---

## Key Takeaway

Elon Musk's ownership of approximately **93.6% of Class B shares** (pre-offering) translates to **~85%+ of total voting power**, giving him unilateral control over board composition, the removal provisions around his own roles, charter amendments affecting the supervoting class, and effectively all major corporate decisions. Public Class A shareholders have severely limited ability to influence governance.

# Controlled-Company Disclosure Summary

Below is a comprehensive synthesis of the controlled-company disclosure drawn from the prospectus excerpts you provided.

---

## 1. Definition of a Controlled Company

Under the listing rules of **Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas**, a company is a **"controlled company"** if more than **50% of the voting power** with respect to director elections is held by **another person or group of persons acting together**. A controlled company may elect not to comply with certain corporate governance requirements applicable to other listed companies.

---

## 2. Controlling Shareholder: Elon Musk

**Elon Musk** is identified as the controlling shareholder. Following the offering:

- He will beneficially own a majority of the outstanding shares of **Class B common stock** (approximately **\[   \]%** of Class B shares).
- He will hold approximately **\[   \]%** of the **total voting power** of the company's common stock (or approximately \[   \]% if the underwriters exercise their option to purchase additional Class A shares in full), through his combined holdings of Class A and Class B common stock.
- He serves as **Chief Executive Officer, Chief Technical Officer, and Chairman of the Board**.
- Under the charter, he **can only be removed** from the board and these leadership positions by the affirmative vote of the holders of a majority of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class.

---

## 3. Dual-Class Share Structure and Board Election Mechanics

The company has a **dual-class common stock structure**:

| Feature | Class A Common Stock | Class B Common Stock |
|---|---|---|
| **Votes per share** | 1 vote per share | 10 votes per share |
| **Conversion** | — | Generally converts to Class A upon transfer (subject to exceptions for inter-family transfers and transfers to "permitted transferees") |

### Board Composition

- Upon consummation of the offering, the board will consist of **eight (8) directors**.
- The board size is determined from time to time by the board itself, subject to the charter and bylaws.

### Director Election Structure

The charter creates a **two-tier director election mechanism**:

| Director Category | How Elected | Number Elected | Initial Members |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class B Directors** | Elected by holders of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class | **51% of total authorized directors, rounded up** to the nearest whole number | Mr. Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, Antonio J. Gracias, Donald Harrison, and Luke Nosek |
| **Common Stock Directors** | Elected by holders of Class A and Class B common stock, voting together as a single class | Remaining directors | Ira Ehrenpreis, Randy Glein, and Steve Jurvetson |

### Additional Governance Provisions

- **Annual elections**: The board is subject to annual elections; each director serves until the next annual meeting (or earlier death, resignation, or removal).
- **No cumulative voting**: The charter does not permit cumulative voting in director elections.
- **Special meetings**: May be called by the chairman, CEO, president (as required by TBOC), the board, the founder, or shareholders holding not less than **50%** of outstanding capital stock entitled to vote.
- **Written consent**: Actions may be taken without a meeting if consented to in writing by holders of sufficient votes, including a separate written consent mechanism for Class B holders acting as a class.
- **Advance notice requirements**: The bylaws establish advance notice procedures for shareholder proposals and director nominations.

### Concentration of Voting Power — Practical Effect

Because Mr. Musk holds a majority of Class B shares (which elect 51% of the board) and a majority of combined voting power (which elects the remaining directors), **he can elect, remove, or fill vacancies among all directors**. This concentration of voting power limits or precludes the ability of Class A shareholders to influence corporate matters and the election of directors, and may have the effect of deferring, delaying, or discouraging hostile takeovers or changes in control.

---

## 4. Independent Directors

The board has determined that the following **five directors** are **independent** under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing standards:

1. **Ira Ehrenpreis**
2. **Randy Glein**
3. **Donald Harrison**
4. **Steve Jurvetson**
5. **Luke Nosek**

In making these determinations, the board considered each director's beneficial ownership of the company's capital stock (and that of affiliated investment funds/entities), relationships disclosed under "Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions," and the fact that Donald Harrison was employed by an organization that does business with the company (though amounts received did not exceed the greater of $200,000 or 5% of either entity's revenues in any of the last three fiscal years).

This means that **three directors are not classified as independent**: Mr. Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, and Antonio J. Gracias. Notably, five of eight directors are independent, which is a **majority** — meaning the company *de facto* meets the majority-independent-board standard even though it is exempt from that requirement.

---

## 5. Exemptions Claimed Under Controlled-Company Status

The company states it **intends to utilize certain exemptions** available to controlled companies. The specific exemptions from Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements are:

### Exemptions the Company Will Rely On

| Exemption | Standard Requirement (for non-controlled companies) | Company's Intended Practice |
|---|---|---|
| **Majority independent board** | A majority of the board must consist of independent directors | The company is exempt; however, 5 of 8 directors are currently independent |
| **Independent nominating committee** | Director nominees must be selected/recommended by a nominating committee composed **entirely of independent directors** with a written charter | The compensation and nominating committee is **not** composed entirely of independent directors (Antonio J. Gracias, who is not independent, is a member) and does **not** have a charter that addresses all Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas requirements |
| **Independent compensation committee** | Compensation committee must be composed **entirely of independent directors** with a written charter | Same as above — the combined compensation and nominating committee does not meet this standard |
| **Annual committee evaluations** | Annual performance evaluations of compensation and nominating committees must be conducted | The company has not indicated it will conduct these evaluations |

### Exemptions the Company Will **Not** Rely On

- **Audit committee independence**: The company will remain subject to rules requiring an **audit committee composed entirely of independent directors**. The initial audit committee members (Randy Glein and Steve Jurvetson) are both independent. A third independent member will be identified within the applicable one-year transition period under Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas rules.

---

## 6. Post-IPO Committees

| Committee | Expected Members | Independent? | Chair | Charter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Audit Committee** | Randy Glein, Steve Jurvetson (third member to be identified within one year) | Yes — both qualify under Rule 10A-3 and Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas standards; Randy Glein expected to qualify as "audit committee financial expert" | Randy Glein | To be available at www.spacex.com |
| **Compensation and Nominating Committee** | Ira Ehrenpreis, Antonio J. Gracias, Luke Nosek | **No** — relies on controlled company exemption; Ehrenpreis and Nosek are independent (including under heightened compensation committee standards and as "non-employee directors" under Rule 16b-3), but Gracias is not | Ira Ehrenpreis | To be available at www.spacex.com; will **not** address all Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas requirements applicable to non-controlled companies |

### Historical Compensation Practices

Before the offering, the company was not publicly traded and had no formal compensation committee. The board historically determined Mr. Musk's compensation. For other executive officers, Mr. Musk had primary responsibility for compensation decisions, though all equity awards were approved by the board.

---

## 7. Shareholder Protections and Risk Disclosure

The prospectus repeatedly warns investors of the **reduced protections** associated with controlled-company status:

> *"Our Class A shareholders will not have the same protections afforded to shareholders of companies that are subject to all of the corporate governance requirements of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas."*

> *"This will limit or preclude your ability to influence corporate matters and the election of our directors."*

Key risk factors highlighted include:

- **Limited shareholder influence**: The dual-class structure and Mr. Musk's controlling position mean Class A shareholders cannot effectively influence corporate governance or board composition.
- **No dividends anticipated**: The company does not plan to pay dividends for the foreseeable future; returns depend on share price appreciation.
- **Potential future additional exemptions**: The company may elect to take advantage of additional controlled-company exemptions in the future.

### Transition on Ceasing to Be a Controlled Company

If the company ceases to be a controlled company and its shares remain listed, it will be required to comply with **all applicable governance requirements within the applicable transition periods**.

---

## 8. Related Corporate Governance Protections (Charter and Bylaws)

Several charter and bylaw provisions reinforce the controlled company's anti-takeover posture:

- **No cumulative voting** in director elections.
- **High threshold for special meetings**: Shareholders holding at least **50%** of outstanding voting shares must call a special meeting.
- **Written consent provisions**: Require the same minimum voting threshold as would be needed at a meeting.
- **Advance notice requirements** for shareholder proposals and director nominations.
- **TBOC provisions adopted**: Including the ability for the company to request a court determination of director independence/disinterestedness at the start of a transaction or derivative claim inquiry; statutory presumptions codifying the **business judgment rule** (requiring proof of fraud, intentional misconduct, or ultra vires acts to overcome).
- **Minimum stock ownership requirements**: Under TBOC § 21.373, shareholders seeking to submit proposals must satisfy ownership, holding-period, and solicitation requirements (including a **3% ownership threshold**), potentially limiting shareholder proposals.
- **Musk's removal**: Can only be accomplished by a majority vote of Class B shareholders, voting as a class.

Based on the provided preamble sections, here is a detailed summary of the director election mechanics and significant shareholder nomination rights for SpaceX:

### **Director Election Mechanics & Board Structure**

*   **Voting Power & Control:** Elon Musk will hold a majority of the voting power through his ownership of Class B common stock (which carries 10 votes per share). This concentration of voting power allows him to control the election of all directors.
*   **Dual-Class Election Process:**
    *   **Class B Directors (51%):** Holders of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, are entitled to elect 51% of the total authorized board seats (rounded up). These directors can be removed only by a majority vote of Class B shareholders.
    *   **Common Stock Directors (49%):** Holders of all voting classes (A and B), voting together as a single class, elect the remaining directors.
*   **Voting Standard:** The documents do not explicitly state whether directors are elected by a plurality or majority vote, but they confirm that **cumulative voting is not permitted**.
*   **Board Terms & Staggering:** All directors will be subject to **annual elections**. There is no mention of a staggered (classified) board.
*   **Controlling Shareholder's Power:** As the holder of a majority of Class B shares, Elon Musk has the power to:
    *   Elect, remove, and fill vacancies for all Class B Directors.
    *   Control the outcome of all matters requiring shareholder approval, including the election of the Common Stock Directors.
    *   Serve as Chairman and CEO with a protected position; his removal requires a separate majority vote of Class B shareholders.

### **Nomination Rights & Procedures**

*   **Advance Notice Bylaws:** The company's bylaws will establish advance notice procedures for shareholders to propose business or nominate director candidates at a meeting.
*   **Exemption for Controller:** **Mr. Musk and his permitted transferees are specifically exempted** from these advance notice requirements.
*   **Lack of Independent Committee:** As a "controlled company," SpaceX will **not** be required to have a nominating committee composed entirely of independent directors or to have a written committee charter. The company intends to rely on this exemption.
*   **Special Meeting Rights:** While the chairman, CEO, president, or the board can call special meetings, **shareholders holding at least 50% of the voting stock** also have the right to call a special meeting.
*   **Written Consent:** Actions can be taken by shareholders without a meeting via written consent signed by the minimum number of holders required to take such action at a meeting.

### **Anti-Takeover Protections (Reinforcing Control)**

The governance structure contains several features that consolidate control and deter hostile takeovers:
*   **No Cumulative Voting:** Prevents minority shareholders from pooling votes to elect a director.
*   **High Threshold for Shareholder Action:** Requiring 50% ownership to call a special meeting and to act by written consent.
*   **"Controlled Company" Status:** Allows the board and its committees to operate without the independent director mandates that provide a check on management for most public companies.
*   **Texas Law Protections:** The charter opts out of certain separate class votes under Texas law and includes provisions governed by the Texas Business Organizations Code (TBOC) that protect against business combinations with affiliated shareholders.

**In summary, the election mechanics and nomination procedures are designed to ensure that Elon Musk, as the controlling shareholder through Class B stock, has decisive control over the board's composition and the company's governance, while Class A public shareholders have significantly limited ability to influence these processes.**

steps.reasoning_content: I now have comprehensive information about SpaceX's dual-class structure, controlled-company status, and board election mechanics. Let me also retrieve information about the economic ownership breakdown - specifically the economic (economic interest/ownership percentage) vs. voting power percentages, to clearly distinguish between governance control and economic ownership.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the ownership table showing the economic ownership percentages (based on shares outstanding or economic interest) versus voting power percentages for Elon Musk and other significant shareholders. I need the pre-offering and post-offering beneficial ownership table that shows number of shares, percentage of shares outstanding, and percentage of voting power for each class and combined. Also retrieve any information about the economic interest or economic rights of Class A versus Class B shareholders including dividend rights, liquidation preferences, and whether all classes share equally in economic distributions.

results.content:
# SpaceX S-1 Prospectus: Ownership Structure & Economic Rights Analysis

## Pre-Offering Beneficial Ownership Table

| Name | Class A Shares | Class A % | Class B Shares | Class B % | Combined Voting Power |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Elon Musk (1)** | 849,494,440 | 12.3% | 5,569,053,075 | 93.6% | **85.1%** |
| Gwynne Shotwell (2) | 5,460,400 | * | 7,113,550 | * | * |
| Bret Johnsen (3) | 9,583,690 | * | — | * | * |
| Ira Ehrenpreis (4) | 809,050 | * | 564,650 | * | * |
| Randy Glein (5) | 277,800 | * | — | * | * |
| Antonio J. Gracias (6) | 503,414,530 | 7.3% | — | * | * |
| Donald Harrison | — | * | — | * | * |
| Steve Jurvetson | — | * | — | * | * |
| Luke Nosek (7) | 32,987,360 | * | — | * | * |

*Represents beneficial ownership or voting power of less than 1%*

**Pre-Offering Share Base:**
- Class A common stock outstanding: **6,932,508,000 shares**
- Class B common stock outstanding: **5,602,790,410 shares**

---

## Post-Offering Beneficial Ownership Table

The document contains blank percentage fields for post-offering figures (indicated by " %"), reflecting that the actual offering size, price, and resulting share counts were not yet finalized at the time of filing. However, the structure shows:

| Name | Class A Shares | Class A % | Class B Shares | Class B % | Combined Voting Power |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Elon Musk (1)** | [blank] | % | [blank] | % | **%** |
| Gwynne Shotwell (2) | [blank] | % | [blank] | % | % |
| [Additional executives...] | [blank] | % | [blank] | % | % |

**Post-Offering Share Base:**
- Class A common stock: **[shares to be determined based on offering]**
- Class B common stock: **[shares to be determined]**

---

## Elon Musk's Shareholdings Composition (Footnote 1 Detail)

Elon Musk's beneficial ownership comprises:

| Component | Class A | Class B |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| (i) Restricted Class B common stock (performance-based vesting) | — | 1,302,072,285 |
| (ii) Elon Musk Revocable Trust dated July 22, 2003 | 842,091,670 | 3,788,654,145 |
| (iii) Musk 2017 Sprinkling Trust dated 12/12/2017 | — | 900,495 |
| (iv) EM 2024 GRAT-A (dated November 26, 2024) | 7,402,770 | — |
| (v) Mission Trust dated December 12, 2019 | — | 127,426,150 |
| (vi) Options exercisable within 60 days of May 1, 2026 | — | 350,000,000 |
| **Total** | **849,494,440** | **5,569,053,075** |

Additionally: 237,530 shares of Class A common stock pledged as security for personal indebtedness.

---

## Voting Power Mechanics

### Dual-Class Voting Structure

| Share Class | Votes Per Share | Board Election Rights |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Class A Common Stock** | 1 vote per share | Elects 49% of directors |
| **Class B Common Stock** | **10 votes per share** | Elects **51% of directors** (rounded up) |
| **Class C Common Stock** | No voting rights | No director election rights |

### Key Control Provisions

**Board Composition:**
- Class B shareholders voting separately elect 51% of authorized directors
- Combined voting determines remaining 49% of directors

**Supermajority/Class Vote Requirements for:**
1. Removal of Mr. Musk from board and leadership roles (CEO, Chairman) — requires majority of Class B voting power
2. Amendment to charter affecting Class B rights, powers, preferences, or privileges
3. Certain combinations, mergers, or similar transactions

**Anti-Takeover Provisions:**
- No cumulative voting permitted
- Special meetings require 50%+ ownership threshold
- Written consent actions require minimum statutory threshold
- Subject to Texas Business Organizations Code § 21.606 (3-year moratorium on business combinations with affiliated shareholders)

---

## Economic Interest Comparison: Class A vs. Class B Shareholders

### Information Available in Document

The S-1 filing **does not explicitly detail** separate economic rights for Class A versus Class B common stock regarding:

| Economic Right | Class A | Class B | Document Clarity |
|---------------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| **Dividend rights** | Not explicitly stated | Not explicitly stated | Unclear |
| **Liquidation preference** | Not explicitly stated | Not explicitly stated | Unclear |
| **Equal economic distribution** | Implied equal | Implied equal | Implied only |

### What IS Specified

**Authorized Capital Structure Post-IPO:**

| Share Class | Authorized Shares | Par Value | Issued/Outstanding |
|-------------|------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Class A Common | 36,132,150,000 | $0.001 | [Per offering] |
| Class B Common | 6,125,000,000 | $0.001 | [Per offering] |
| Class C Common | 10,000,000,000 | $0.001 | None issued |
| Preferred Stock | 2,400,000,000 | $0.001 | None issued |

### Conversion Mechanics

**Class B → Class A Conversion Triggers:**
1. **Voluntary conversion:** At holder's option, at any time
2. **Automatic conversion:** Upon any sale, transfer, or disposition of Class B shares (or beneficial interest), except for "Permitted Transfers"
3. **Permitted Transfers:** Certain inter-family transfers and transfers to qualifying entities

**Effect of Conversions:**
> "The conversion of Class B common stock to Class A common stock will have the effect, over time, of increasing the relative voting power of those holders of Class B common stock who retain their shares."

This suggests a mechanism where Class B voting power concentrates over time as shares convert to Class A upon market sale.

---

## Dilution Analysis Table

The document includes a dilution table showing ownership distribution between existing and new investors:

| Investor Group | Shares Acquired | % | Total Consideration | % | Avg Price/Share |
|---------------|----------------|---|-------------------|---|----------------|
| Elon Musk & existing investors | — | % | $ | % | $ |
| New investors (this offering) | — | % | $ | % | $ |
| **Total** | — | **100.0%** | $ | **100.0%** | $ |

*(Blank fields indicate final figures depend on offering price and size)*

**Sensitivity Note:** Each $1.00 increase/decrease in offering price increases/decreases total consideration proportionally.

---

## Controlled Company Status

Following the IPO, SpaceX will qualify as a **"controlled company"** under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas rules because:

1. Mr. Musk will beneficially own a majority of Class B voting power
2. Class B holders elect 51% of the board

**Exemptions Available:**
- No requirement for majority independent board
- No requirement for independent compensation committee
- No requirement for independent nominating committee
- **Must maintain:** Independent audit committee (per SEC requirements)

---

## Summary: Economic vs. Voting Power Discrepancy

| Metric | Elon Musk's Position |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Class A Economic Ownership** | 12.3% of Class A shares |
| **Class B Ownership** | 93.6% of Class B shares |
| **Combined Voting Power** | 85.1% |
| **Implied Economic Interest** | Significantly lower than voting power |

The **critical distinction** evident from this filing is the extreme separation between:
- **Economic ownership** (share count/percentage) — where Musk holds ~12.3% of Class A
- **Voting control** — where Musk commands ~85.1% combined voting power through Class B super-voting shares

This creates a structure where a minority economic stake provides majority control over corporate governance decisions, board composition, and strategic direction.

---

## Document Limitations

The following specific data points appear in the document but contain blank/placeholder values:

1. Post-offering share counts for all individuals
2. Post-offering percentage ownership for all individuals  
3. Exact number of Class A shares offered
4. Final offering price
5. Explicit dividend or liquidation preference differentials between share classes

These blanks were likely intentional at the filing stage, to be completed with final offering terms. The document confirms that Class A and Class B share equal economic rights as common stock, with the **only differentiation being voting power** (1 vote vs. 10 votes) and **board election rights** (Class B's guaranteed 51% board representation).

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have all the information I need to provide a comprehensive answer distinguishing between governance control and economic ownership using SpaceX's dual-class structure and board-election mechanics. Let me synthesize this into a clear, well-structured response.

arguments.final_result:
# Governance Control vs. Economic Ownership in SpaceX's Dual-Class Structure

## The Core Distinction

SpaceX's S-1 filing reveals a stark separation between **governance control** (the power to direct corporate decisions and board composition) and **economic ownership** (the right to share proportionally in the company's financial value). These two dimensions are deliberately decoupled through the dual-class share structure.

---

## 1. The Dual-Class Mechanism: How Control and Economics Diverge

### Voting Rights Per Share

| Share Class | Votes Per Share | Economic Interest Per Share |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| **Class A** | 1 vote | Equal (1 share = 1 economic unit) |
| **Class B** | **10 votes** (supervoting) | Equal (1 share = 1 economic unit) |
| **Class C** | 0 votes (non-voting) | Equal (1 share = 1 economic unit) |

The filing does **not** disclose any dividend preference, liquidation preference, or economic subordination between the three common stock classes. All classes appear to share equally in economic distributions. The **sole differentiator is voting power**: Class B carries 10× the voting power of Class A on a per-share basis.

### The Resulting Asymmetry (Pre-Offering)

| Metric | Elon Musk's Position | Interpretation |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| Class A shares owned | 849,494,440 (12.3% of Class A) | Minority economic claim on the larger, liquid class |
| Class B shares owned | 5,569,053,075 (93.6% of Class B) | Near-total ownership of the supervoting class |
| Combined economic interest | ~47% of total shares outstanding* | Plurality but not majority economic interest |
| **Combined voting power** | **85.1%** | **Supermajority governance control** |

*Approximate: Musk holds 6,418,547,515 shares out of ~12,535,298,410 total shares outstanding pre-offering (6,932,508,000 Class A + 5,602,790,410 Class B).

**Key takeaway:** Musk's 10-vote Class B shares convert a ~47% economic stake into 85.1% voting power—meaning he commands governance decisions with nearly twice the proportional authority that his economic ownership would otherwise warrant.

---

## 2. Board-Election Mechanics: Where Governance Control Is Institutionalized

The charter creates a **two-track director election system** that goes beyond simple voting-power concentration:

### Track 1 — Class B Directors (51% of the Board)

- **Elected by:** Holders of Class B common stock, voting **separately as a class**
- **Seats:** 51% of authorized directors, rounded up (for an 8-person board = **5 seats**)
- **Removal:** Only by affirmative vote of a majority of Class B shareholders voting as a separate class
- **Initial members:** Elon Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, Antonio Gracias, Donald Harrison, Luke Nosek

### Track 2 — Common Stock Directors (49% of the Board)

- **Elected by:** Holders of all voting classes (A and B), voting **together as a single class**
- **Seats:** Remaining directors (for an 8-person board = **3 seats**)
- **Initial members:** Ira Ehrenpreis, Randy Glein, Steve Jurvetson

### Practical Effect of the Two-Track System

Because Musk holds 93.6% of Class B shares, he independently controls:
- The election of all **5 Class B Director seats** (Track 1)
- The outcome of all combined votes for the remaining **3 Common Stock Director seats** (Track 2, given his 85.1% combined voting power)

**Result:** Musk unilaterally controls **100% of board seats** despite owning only ~47% of total economic interests.

Additionally, Musk's removal from the board, CEO, and Chairman roles can **only** be accomplished by a majority vote of Class B shareholders voting as a separate class—a provision that effectively makes his position self-perpetuating given his 93.6% Class B ownership.

---

## 3. Controlled-Company Status: The Governance Exemption Layer

Because Musk holds a majority of voting power with respect to director elections, SpaceX qualifies as a **"controlled company"** under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules. This adds a second layer of governance-economic distinction:

### Exemptions Claimed

| Governance Requirement | Standard Rule | SpaceX's Exemption |
|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Majority-independent board | Required | **Exempt** (though 5 of 8 directors are independently determined to be independent) |
| Independent compensation committee | Required | **Exempt** (committee includes non-independent member Antonio Gracias) |
| Independent nominating committee | Required | **Exempt** (same combined committee) |
| Independent audit committee | Required | **Not exempt** — must comply (2 of 3 initial members independent) |

### What This Means for Economic Stakeholders

Class A shareholders—the primary economic participants post-IPO—are denied:
1. Board representation proportional to their economic interest
2. The governance protections (independent committees, majority-independent board) that typically constrain management in public companies
3. Any practical ability to influence director elections, given the mathematical impossibility of overcoming Musk's 85.1% voting power
4. The ability to remove Musk from leadership, since that requires a Class B-only vote

---

## 4. Additional Governance Control Reinforcements

Several charter and bylaw provisions further consolidate governance control beyond what the dual-class structure alone achieves:

| Provision | Effect on Governance Control |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| **No cumulative voting** | Prevents minority shareholders from pooling votes to elect even one director |
| **50% threshold for special meetings** | Class A shareholders cannot call special meetings without Musk's support |
| **Advance notice requirements** | Restricts shareholder proposals and director nominations (Musk exempted from advance notice) |
| **TBOC § 21.606** | 3-year moratorium on business combinations with "affiliated shareholders" absent board pre-approval |
| **3% ownership threshold** | Under TBOC § 21.373, limits shareholder proposal submissions |

---

## 5. The Erosion Mechanism: Class B Conversion Dynamics

The charter contains an important temporal dynamic that further separates governance control from economic ownership over time:

- **Automatic conversion:** Each Class B share converts to Class A upon any transfer (sale, gift, etc.), except for limited "Permitted Transfers" (inter-family, qualifying trusts)
- **Voluntary conversion:** At holder's option at any time

The filing explicitly notes:
> *"The conversion of Class B common stock to Class A common stock will have the effect, over time, of increasing the relative voting power of those holders of Class B common stock who retain their shares."*

This means:
1. As Class B holders sell shares (converting to Class A), the **pool of supervoting shares shrinks**
2. Holders who retain Class B shares see their **relative voting power increase** even without buying more shares
3. Economic ownership becomes more dispersed (via Class A market sales) while governance control becomes more concentrated

---

## 6. Summary: The Governance-Economic Gap

| Dimension | Governance Control | Economic Ownership |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Elon Musk's position** | 85.1% voting power; controls 100% of board | ~47% of total shares; minority of publicly traded Class A |
| **Mechanism** | 10-vote Class B shares + 51% Class B board seats + separate-class voting rights | Equal economic interest per share across all common classes |
| **Public Class A shareholders** | ~15% voting power collectively; cannot elect any directors or influence governance | Majority of liquid, publicly traded economic interest |
| **Board protections** | Musk's removal requires Class B-only majority vote | No economic preference or priority over Class B |
| **Committee protections** | Controlled-company exemption eliminates independent committee requirements | N/A |
| **Temporal trajectory** | Governance control concentrates as retained Class B holders' relative power increases | Economic ownership disperses as Class B converts to Class A upon sale |

**Conclusion:** SpaceX's charter deliberately constructs a system where governance rights are disproportionately allocated to Class B holders (primarily Musk) while economic rights remain nominally equal across share classes. The controlled-company exemption further strips public economic participants of the institutional governance safeguards that normally accompany public equity ownership. The result is a structure in which the economic risk is borne broadly by public shareholders, but governance authority is concentrated narrowly in the hands of the controlling shareholder—a classic feature of controlled dual-class companies, executed through SpaceX's specific charter provisions with particular intensity given the 10:1 supervoting ratio and the 51%-Class-B-director floor.

---

**Sources:**
- SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm
- Sections reviewed: Beneficial Ownership Table, Description of Capital Stock, Corporate Governance, Risk Factors, Controlled-Company Disclosure

- **Seats:** 51% of authorized directors, rounded up (for an 8-person board = **5 seats**)

## 3. Controlled-Company Status: The Governance Exemption Layer

Because Musk holds a majority of voting power with respect to director elections, SpaceX qualifies as a **"controlled company"** under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules. This adds a second layer of governance-economic distinction:

| Provision | Effect on Governance Control |

| Dimension | Governance Control | Economic Ownership |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Elon Musk's position** | 85.1% voting power; controls 100% of board | ~47% of total shares; minority of publicly traded Class A |
| **Mechanism** | 10-vote Class B shares + 51% Class B board seats + separate-class voting rights | Equal economic interest per share across all common classes |
